summary: I was born on Washington Street in Virginia City, Nevada, on April 6, 1910. Virginia City was a booming mining town when my grandfather first arrived in 1873, and it has always been central to my family's history. The town's vibrant activity and beautiful surroundings made it a special place for us. Many important places like the Storey County Hospital and the Flowery district are nearby, shaping much of my early life.

Storey County is where Virginia City is located, and it's been home to my family for generations. My grandfather settled in this county during the mining boom in the 1870s, and we lived in areas like Six Mile and Seven Mile Canyons within Storey County. The county had important landmarks such as the Storey County Hospital and the Schnitzer Brewery, which were part of the community life I knew growing up.

Fred Hodgins was the doctor who delivered me on Washington Street in Virginia City back in 1910. I remember him as the medical professional who helped bring me into this world. His role was important to my family, marking the beginning of my life in this historic town.

My grandfather, Thomas James Berry, was the first of my mother's family to come to the Comstock area. He arrived from England in 1873 during Virginia City's boom days and was captivated by the beauty and activity of the region. He initially worked as a miner and later built a home in Six Mile Canyon with my grandmother. He was known as 'Strawberry' because of his red hair and was quite the entertainer, singing and dancing at local parties.

England is where my grandfather, Thomas James Berry, originally came from before moving to Nevada in 1873. He returned there to marry my grandmother, Sarah Jane Bentham, in a small town called Hulme in Lancashire County. England was the starting point of my family's journey to the Comstock area, and it holds the roots of our heritage.

Six Mile Canyon is a place my grandfather grew fond of when he first came to Virginia City. It’s part of the Flowery district and close to Virginia City, with the Storey County Hospital at its head and the Schnitzer Brewery nearby. My grandfather eventually built a large home there as his family grew, and it was a significant part of our family’s life and history.

The Flowery district is an area east of Virginia City that includes both Six Mile Canyon and Seven Mile Canyon. My grandparents settled here after returning from England, and it was where my grandfather worked in the mines. This district was home to many families and had several important structures like saloons and the hospital, making it a lively community.

Sarah Jane Bentham was my grandmother, whom my grandfather married in Hulme, Lancashire, England. After their marriage, they moved to Virginia City and settled in Seven Mile Canyon within the Flowery district. She was part of the family that grew in this mining community, and I remember stories about her reactions to some of the local cooking and life in the canyons.

Hulme is the small town in Lancashire County, England, where my grandparents were married. After my grandfather first came to Nevada, he returned to Hulme to marry my grandmother, Sarah Jane Bentham, before they moved back to Virginia City. Hulme represents the English roots of my family before they settled in Nevada.

Seven Mile Canyon is where my grandparents first settled after returning from England, located east of Virginia City in the Flowery district. My grandfather worked in the mines there, and the winter was very harsh, with stories of coyotes following him on his walks to work. Although they didn’t stay long, Seven Mile Canyon was an important early home for the family before moving to Six Mile Canyon.

The Schnitzer Brewery was a beautiful old building located in Six Mile Canyon, near the Storey County Hospital. It was well known for making its own beer and was a popular spot for miners and locals after their shifts. Sadly, the brewery burned down in 1983, but it remains a memorable part of the community’s history.

Fanny O'Mare was a petite lady who ran a small saloon further down Six Mile Canyon. She was said to be French and spoke the language fluently, cooking French meals for her friends, though she also had an Irish accent. My grandfather especially liked her leg of lamb with garlic, even though my grandmother didn’t approve of it. Fanny was a colorful character in the community and added to the unique flavor of life in the canyon.

The Carney family operated another small saloon further down Six Mile Canyon, near a natural spring. Their saloon was a gathering place for locals and travelers passing through the area. The presence of the spring made it a convenient and popular spot, contributing to the social life of the Flowery district community.
